sections.title: Meat physics & selection
sections.subtitle: Water · grades · flat inspection

items.text: Raw brisket is ~70% water — stored primarily inside muscle fibres, not in the fat cap.
Higher marbling (Prime, BMS 5–7+, MSA 400+) lubricates slices but does not replace moisture lost to overcook.
USDA grade is scored on the ribeye (12th/13th rib), not the brisket. Inspect the flat through the wrap for thin white fat striations; solid red beef is lean regardless of the sticker.
Pick your regional sticker in Weight — the app maps it to broad bands: low, moderate, high, or extreme / Wagyu-grade. Not exact 1:1 across countries.
Plan 30–40% cook shrink; even a juicy cook can approach ~43% loss on the scale.

sections.callout: Yield tab — model trim + moisture + render from start weight.

sections.title: Evaporative stall
sections.subtitle: 65.5 – 74 °C internal

items.text: Between 65.5 °C and 74 °C (150–165 °F) the brisket “sweats” — evaporative cooling fights your pit temp.
Bump pit temperature or airflow to power through; sitting in the stall too long costs moisture.
“Stall rescue” preset (~76.5 °C pull/hold) is for planning around a stuck cook — not the juicy long-hold finish.

sections.callout: Dashboard timeline marks the stall band — calculator uses the same temp range.

sections.title: Exponential rendering model
sections.subtitle: Time × temperature · additive hours

items.text: Collagen → gelatin is exponential: rendering = time multiplied by temperature, not a linear “hours per pound” clock.
At each steady internal temp, every hour adds that row’s “% rendered per hour” to your running total (see Reference table).
100% = ideal tenderness in this model. Acceptable slice window: 80–120% total rendering.
Pull at 90.5 °C (~195 °F) ≈ 40% rendered and probe-tight; finish the remaining ~60% in a 65.5 °C (150 °F) hot box — often ~18 hr.
Above 93.3 °C (200 °F), fibres denature fast and squeeze moisture like a sponge — why 95 °C (203 °F) on smoke alone is risky.

sections.callout: Pull planner slider = render built if you pulled now · Hold box finishes the rest.

items.text: Aerodynamic trim: remove the “mohawk” flap at an angle so the pack sits flat on the grate.
Fat cap exactly 0.6 cm (¼ in) — thicker cap stays opaque and unrendered; trim ~1.3 cm (½ in) from sides to read the cap.
Half-moon cut: one continuous curved slice to round the thin flat end — stops corner “jerky”.
Dry brine with table salt first for deeper penetration; coarse pepper/kosher on top for bark.
Sumac (ground Middle Eastern berry) in the rub gives dark, tangy bark without coffee-bitter notes.
Plastic wrap overnight in the fridge — blocks the fan from drying the surface while salt brines under a moist film.
Fire basket: hotter, cleaner coal bed with less babysitting on long cooks.
Offset smokers: mostly convective heat (air moves top-down). Pellet grills: more radiant from below.
On pellets, use the top rack with a water pan below to blunt radiant heat on the flat.
Stack dropped to grate level helps even chamber temperature left-to-right.
Stack extension pulls more oxygen through the fire for a cleaner burn and steadier draft.

sections.title: Smoke, ring & surface chemistry
sections.subtitle: Myths vs what still happens on the pit

items.text: 60 °C (140 °F) smoke myth busted: brisket keeps taking on smoke flavour well past 140 °F — the ring is not the flavour stop.
Smoke ring can continue forming up to ~76.5 °C (170 °F) on a wet, airflow-friendly surface.
Wetter bark (spritz, humidity) grabs more smoke and often a wider ring — not a substitute for good fire.
Temperature = molecular motion; heat = energy transfer into the meat — both matter for bark and render.

sections.title: Probing, carry-over & food safety
sections.subtitle: Before you trust the dial

items.text: Learn landmark probe feel from ~82 °C (180 °F): glide-in “room-temp butter” on the flat — not mush, not cold push-back.
Covered water-pan test on the oven’s lowest setting — residential dials often read 15+ °F high.
Pulled hot at 95 °C (203 °F)? Counter-rest ~2 hr until internal slides toward ~65 °C before cambro hold — otherwise carry-over turns it mush.
Food-safe pasteurisation from ~55 °C (131 °F); for long hot holds use 60–65.5 °C so tenderization stays slow and safe.

sections.callout: Rest & carry-over tab — hour-by-hour temp and extra render %.

sections.title: Advanced cook methods
sections.subtitle: Foil boat · long hold · confit · reverse

items.text: Foil boat (beginner): 121–135 °C (250–275 °F) smoke 4–5 hr until bark is dry and firm → open foil boat → pit 149 °C (300 °F) — bottom braises, fat cap renders on top.

Undercook & long hold: pull 90.5 °C (~40% rendered, tight probe) → straight into 65.5 °C × ~18 hr; ~4 hr cool-down from 90.5 → 65.5 °C finishes most of the remaining render without 93 °C+ squeeze-out.
Brisket confit: smoke to ~74 °C → submerge in 68.3 °C (155 °F) tallow (sous-vide) × 18 hr — prime-rib-like texture.
Reverse smoke: butcher paper on first → 88 °C → refrigerate → reheat on smoker at 121 °C until ~65.5 °C internal — juicier, less total smoke.

sections.callout: Techniques tab — numbered step lists for each method.
